transcription:
[figure]

Timing faults
don't really apply
for asynchronous
network models, where
messages take arbitrarily
long to deliver.

Omission faults and crash faults are the main
subjects in this class

[figure]

Authentication
detectable Byzantine
faults can
be downgraded
to omission
faults (drop
the messages)

☆ crash model = crash faults may occur
☆ omission model = omission and crash faults may occur

In the omission fault model, two participants can't
reach an agreement (two general's)

"common knowledge" solves this
  ↳
  - everyone knows P
  - everyone knows that everyone knows P
  - everyone knows ... everyone knows P
        infinite
The more message you receive, the more confidence
a process have, but there is no certainty.

Reliable delivery: If not <u>all</u> messages are lost, the
message will be eventually delivered.

To send a messages: (reliable delivery)
  - put it in a send buffer
  - After a given timeout send what's in the
  buffer
  - when an ack is received, delete it from
  the buffer

  To receive and deliver a message:
  - Ack the sender
(At least once delivery)

<u>Some messages which change state</u> would
mess up state if duplicated

<u>Idempotent</u> messages can be delivered multiple
times

Exactly-once delivery? Only idempotent messages
— message de-duplication with metadata

Robust design is easier with idempotent messages

Omission faults and crash faults are the main subjects in this class
Authentication detectable Byzantine faults can be downgraded to omission faults (drop the messages)

☆ omission model = omission and crash faults may occur

After a given timeout send what's in the buffer
when an ack is received, delete it from the buffer
